title: Tremé Black Culture, Brass & Procession
summary: Follow Tremé through second lines, brass-band memory, Black cultural institutions, sacred history, and the neighborhood traditions that make New Orleans unmistakable.

stops.title: Backstreet Cultural Museum
stops.description: Open with a museum that centers second lines, Mardi Gras Indians, and the Black neighborhood practices that keep Tremé's cultural life in motion.

stops.title: Tremé's Petit Jazz Museum
stops.description: Keep the route intimate and neighborhood-rooted at a stop where jazz history stays local, lived, and close to the block.

stops.title: New Orleans African American Museum
stops.description: Bring in public interpretation, preservation, and Black historical stewardship at one of Tremé's strongest institutional anchors.

stops.title: St. Augustine Church
stops.description: Add spiritual memory and the deeper sacred history of Black New Orleans at a church that expands the route beyond performance alone.

stops.title: Mahalia Jackson Theater
stops.description: Close with a civic-scale performance anchor that links neighborhood cultural practice to the larger public stage of New Orleans.

title: Congo Square, Freedom & Black New Orleans Origins
summary: Move through Congo Square, Armstrong Park, sacred memory, and the Tremé corridor to read the deepest Black origins of New Orleans in public space.

stops.title: Congo Square
stops.description: Open where Black gathering, retention, rhythm, and survival formed one of the deepest cultural foundations in New Orleans.

stops.title: Louis Armstrong Park
stops.description: Expand the route from the square into a broader public memory landscape tied to Black cultural origins and civic recognition.

stops.title: St. Augustine Church
stops.description: Bring sacred history and freedom memory into the route through one of Tremé's most resonant Black religious sites.

stops.title: New Orleans African American Museum
stops.description: Add public interpretation and Black historical stewardship at an institution that helps hold the city's deeper story together.

stops.title: North Claiborne Corridor
stops.description: Close with a corridor that bridges Black origins and freedom memory into the longer neighborhood history of Tremé and Claiborne.

title: Claiborne, Neighborhood Memory & Urban Change
summary: Read Claiborne through neighborhood institutions, corridor repair, Bayou Road commerce, and the Black civic spaces that held Tremé together through disruption and change.

stops.title: Dooky Chase's Restaurant
stops.description: Open with a neighborhood institution where food, civil-rights strategy, and Black New Orleans public life met across generations.

stops.title: Tremé Recreation Community Center
stops.description: Move into the everyday civic life of Tremé at a community anchor that keeps the route grounded in neighborhood use, not only memory.

stops.title: Claiborne Corridor Cultural Innovation District
stops.description: Read the underpass and corridor itself as a site of loss, repair, and Black-led cultural reactivation along North Claiborne.

stops.title: Community Book Center
stops.description: Add literary memory and Black-owned continuity at a bookstore that helps Bayou Road remain a living cultural corridor.

stops.title: Bayou Road Black Commercial Corridor
stops.description: Close along Bayou Road, where neighborhood commerce, cultural resilience, and Black local life push the route beyond preservation into ongoing survival.

title: Black New Orleans Arts, Performance & Cultural Institutions
summary: Move through Black-led cultural institutions, jazz stewardship, arts training, and public creative life to see how New Orleans keeps performance and cultural memory in circulation.

stops.title: Ashé Cultural Arts Center
stops.description: Open in Central City at a Black-led cultural institution that ties art directly to community power, diaspora memory, and neighborhood development.

stops.title: Le Musée de f.p.c.
stops.description: Add a house museum dedicated to free people of color, where material culture and Black New Orleans history meet the city’s creative inheritance.

stops.title: New Orleans African American Museum
stops.description: Center Black art, history, and contemporary exhibition practice at an institution that actively preserves and elevates cultural life in Tremé.

stops.title: New Orleans Jazz Museum
stops.description: Move into public music stewardship at a museum that treats jazz as a living art form through exhibits, research, and performance.

stops.title: New Orleans Center for Creative Arts
stops.description: Close with arts training and future-facing creative practice at a school that helps shape the next generation of New Orleans artists and performers.

title: Civil Rights, Resistance & Public Power in New Orleans
summary: Trace Black public power through movement institutions, school desegregation, legal challenge, civil-rights memory, and the civic spaces where New Orleans policy is actually made.

stops.title: Dryades YMCA
stops.description: Open on Oretha Castle Haley at a Black community institution that anchors the route in organizing, gathering, and neighborhood public life.

stops.title: Louisiana Civil Rights Museum
stops.description: Move into statewide memory at a museum that frames New Orleans inside the larger struggle for civil rights across Louisiana.

stops.title: William Frantz School Site
stops.description: Read school desegregation and child-centered courage at the site most associated with Ruby Bridges and the national visibility of New Orleans resistance.

stops.title: Homer Plessy Way
stops.description: Add legal challenge and segregation history at the corridor that remembers Homer Plessy and the case that shaped public life far beyond New Orleans.

stops.title: New Orleans City Hall
stops.description: Close at the city’s governing core, where movement pressure, Black public power, and policy decisions meet in the machinery of local government.
